children.label: Many people are unhappy with Linkedin
LinkedIn inbox is a poor networking tool. Messages go unread, no prioritization, creates noise not signal
children.test: Mom tests across AEs, founders, VCs — inbox usage & frustration
children.reason: Most people didn't feel it's an issue. The ones that have a lot of linkedin reachout use external tools for it anyway.

children.label: Users want to delegate their LinkedIn inbox/outbox (and email) to an SDR or recruiter
children.test: Mom test: when and why do users want to delegate LinkedIn/email communication? What triggers the need and what are current workarounds?

children.label: Mutual connections is inaccurate — they see 300+ but have no intelligence on which paths are real
children.test: Behavioral: how often do people see / click mutual connections? What for?

children.label: Users want a real picture of their own network — active vs dormant connections, response likelihood, and intro balance (favors given vs called) — not a vanity count
children.test: Demo/waitlist test: show a mockup of a network health dashboard — does it generate sign-up intent or 'I want that' reactions? Not a Mom Test — this is a latent need, solution-first.

children.label: Users want to benchmark their network health against peers — how do I compare to my co-founder, or how strong is a friend's network before I ask them for something?
children.test: Pending: validate parent node (network-self-map) first

children.label: Option B: Strategic Reach — find the real intro path to a specific high-value target
children.test: Mom tests — how often do you target a specific person for an intro?
children.reason: Frequency too low — ~2× per Q. Not a recurring daily pain.

children.label: Business leaders in small orgs (<20) are more likely to ask intros
children.test: Analysis on past calls

children.label: Small org Option B: Strategic Reach — find the real intro path to a specific target
children.test: Mom test: how often do small org front-people target a specific person for an intro?

children.label: Waterfall sequencing: try connector #1 → auto-fallback #2, prevent double-ask
children.test: Pending: validate parent node frequency first

children.label: Filter 300+ mutuals to 3–5 real paths via active relationship signals
children.test: Pending: validate parent node frequency first

children.label: Rank connectors by responsiveness to both you and the target
children.test: Pending: validate parent node frequency first

children.label: Users want to copy Gordy on outreach so he can manage the waterfall of warm path attempts automatically
children.test: Mockup test: show the cc-Gordy flow — does the user understand it and want to use it?

children.label: Users will install a chrome extension and connect their calendar in order to get a reality check on their network
children.test: Mockup test: are they willing to install

children.label: Users with chrome extension will send their friends the extension to discover friends network as well
children.test: Mockup test: are they willing to come up with names? intro us?

children.label: Users who received a request to install the chrome extension and connect their calendar will do so.
children.test: MVT test: Send them the emails pretending we already have the flow implemented

children.label: Job searchers are asking for many intros
children.test: Interview: when and why do you look at mutual connections?

children.label: Many mutual connections - helps the user build confidence before the reachout. More chances for him to accept.
children.test: Interview: when and why do you look at mutual connections?

children.label: People check mutual connections as part of understanding the target. What people he associate with.
children.test: Interview: what triggers you to check mutual connections?

children.reason: **The overwhelming majority expressed dissatisfaction with LinkedIn.** Out of approximately 55 unique interviewees, roughly **40+ expressed negative sentiment**, while only about **8-10 spoke favorably**, with the remainder mixed.
children.test: Question all transcripts: Are people unhappy with Linkedin - I need count of people mentioning that vs. people taking favorbly about linkedin. Include names. Skip Yaniv, Matan and Shaul

children.label: HubSpot / newsletter approach to nurturing is broken
AEs will pay out of pocket for personal network CRM — owned by the rep, not the company
children.test: AE validation interviews (simulation + real calls) + cold email signal

children.label: AEs want to own their network and move it with them when they change jobs — their relationships are personal assets, not company property
children.test: Pending: parent node (aes) is eliminated due to WTP blocker. Validate willingness to pay first.

children.label: AEs want to claim attribution for deals they influenced through personal relationships — giving them a concrete reason to nurture outside the company CRM
children.test: Pending: parent node (aes) is eliminated due to WTP blocker. Validate willingness to pay first.

children.label: CSMs need relationship intelligence to prevent churn and surface expansion opportunities
children.test: Cold email signal — real call interviews pending

children.label: Business leaders in small orgs (<20) are more likely to manage relationships and need personal nurturing
children.test: Analysis of past calls

children.label: Founders in small companies that are wearing the sales hat are losing deals because of poor nurturing
children.test: Behavioual: founder interviews

children.label: Users want to stay top of mind so customers call them when in-market and various opportunities find them
children.test: Mom Test: do users find work arounds to stay top of mind. Do they agree that this is the motivation

children.label: VC principals miss deal flow — can't maintain active signals on 80–150 priority founders
children.test: Mom test - what happens after the intro call if it's too early

children.label: Users think that more personal contact intelligence improves nurturing by creating more follow-up opportunities that aren't lame, ultimately improving win rates
children.test: Mom test: do people who know personal details about contacts report better relationship outcomes?

children.label: Users want to leverage personal anecdotes on connectors so they can ask more intros
children.test: Mom test: do they try to make the small talk before the intro request

children.label: Users will pay to mine personal intel from their own emails, call transcripts, and WhatsApp to fuel warm relationship actions
children.test: Mockup test: when offered pokemon mining, do users want it? Send the transcripts? Connect email?

children.label: Users who have gathered deep personal intel about contacts through real relationships are willing to share or trade it with others in exchange for reputation, credits, or reciprocal intel
children.test: Interview: have they shared before? What info are they ok sharing? Who are they willing to share with?

children.label: Users want alerts about life/career trigger events for contacts — job change, funding, promotion, local holiday, local news — as a reason to reach out and nurture
children.test: Mom test: do they actually search for it

children.label: Users want alerts about dynamic data matching their pokemon intel — sports results, news, events — as a zero-effort reason to reach out (the "Sarah's Seahawks won" mechanic)
children.test: Mom test: do users currently track dynamic external data (sports, news, events) about contacts? Do they act on it when they notice it organically?
test

children.label: Introductions & intro-meetings are a viral product space
Business leaders in small orgs (<20) are more likely to ask intros
children.test: Analysis on past callsd

children.label: Asker want to know when a giver makes a new real connection that is relevant to them so they can ask for an intro.
children.test: Mockup test - see if the asker is willing to introduce to giver

children.label: Asker and giver engage frequently in "open the book" exercise and it has friction
children.test: Mom test - Ask both investors and early stage founders the frequency of "open the book" + what challenges

children.label: Asker sees which of helper's connections are real & active before asking - he convinces the give to participate
children.test: Mockup test - Pretending it's ready.  Who will they ask tomorrow.

children.label: Giver is willing to install a tool that shares a real map with the asker
children.test: Mockup test - pretending it's real

children.label: Option C: Meeting Intro Engine — pre-meeting briefs create two-sided value; counterpart email is the viral moment of conversion
children.test: Behavioral: do people practice the idea of asking for 2-3 introductions in every meeting

children.label: Gordy Strategic Ask Brief creates enough value that users share it internally with colleagues
children.test: Internal: we need to bring users from companies with actual employees

children.label: Counterpart email creates desire in someone who's never heard of InTouch — before they have a product to land on
children.test: Live emails to real attendees — measuring clicks, replies, sign-up intent

children.label: Auto compose intro request feature is used and users don't delete the attribution
children.test: Data from live experiments + users interview

children.label: Post-meeting Gordy follow-up converts successful meetings into new warm user acquisition
children.test: Pending: define loop after counterpart email signal confirmed

children.label: Name drop test: "Gordy mentioned you know X — may I mention you when I reach out?" creates warm outreach at scale
children.test: Live Gordy outreach to founders, VCs, AEs — tracking Yes/No + follow-up calls

children.label: Name drop works with founders and VCs — strong positive signal confirmed
children.test: Name drop test run on founder + investor network

children.label: Name drop works with AEs and sales professionals — test running
children.test: Name drop test on AE network — running

children.label: Prospects respond to Gordy outbound offering intros
children.test: Live test: Gordy sends outbound messages offering intros to targeted prospects — measure response rate and yes/no ratio
Past successes
children.reason: Calendly, zoom, Doodle (10M monthly users), Superhuman, Fathom, Firefly, Boardy, ...

children.label: Knowing something personal about a target meaningfully improves cold outreach conversion rates
children.test: A/B test: compare cold outreach with pokemon-sourced personal intel vs generic outreach — does conversion rate differ significantly?

children.label: Business leaders in small orgs (<20) are more likely to research data manually
children.test: Analysis of past calls

children.label: Users doing cold outreach have no scalable way to get public personal intel on targets today — they do it manually or skip it entirely
children.test: Mom test: do people research targets before cold outreach? How long does it take? Have they tried to automate it? Would they pay to get it instantly?

children.label: Users will pay to access personal intel that others have collected about a target they've never met — fueling better cold outreach and first meetings
children.test: Interview: do they feel comfortable with the concept? Show them a mockup — do they want it?

children.label: Users want to avoid data entry
children.test: Validated by market: 25+ predecessor companies failed primarily due to manual data entry abandonment (Contactually, Highrise, Nimble, etc.). Confirmed independently across multiple founder, AE, and VC interviews.
children.reason: Historical pattern across $300M+ in failed CRM attempts. Interviewees consistently report starting tools and abandoning them when upkeep becomes too heavy.
